summary: This PR refactors `LineDecoder` to use Python's `str.splitlines()` for line splitting, which the author claims is a significant speed-up. The main concerns are: (1) `splitlines()` recognises many more line-ending characters than the original `\n`/`\r`/`\r\n`-only implementation, which is a meaningful behavioural change for HTTP/SSE consumers and a potential security concern; (2) the constant `NEWLINE_CHARS` is defined inside the method body on every call; and (3) test coverage does not exercise the newly-supported separators or several edge cases in the buffering logic.

findings.title: splitlines() expands recognised line endings beyond \n/\r/\r\n, introducing behavioural regression and potential security concern
findings.detail:
The new implementation delegates to `str.splitlines()`, which treats `\x0b` (VT), `\x0c` (FF), `\x1c`–`\x1e`, `\x85` (NEL), `\u2028` (LINE SEPARATOR), and `\u2029` (PARAGRAPH SEPARATOR) as line boundaries. The original code — and the docstring — only guaranteed `\n`, `\r`, and `\r\n`. HTTP/1.1 (RFC 7230) and the SSE specification only recognise those three as valid line terminators. Concretely:

• **Behavioural regression**: JSON bodies or other response payloads that legitimately contain `\u2028`/`\u2029` (which are valid in JSON strings) will be incorrectly split into multiple lines.
• **Security concern**: An attacker-controlled response body containing these characters could craft lines that appear whole to the application but are split by the decoder, potentially causing header-injection-style confusion in any line-oriented protocol parser (e.g., SSE `data:` fields) layered on top of `iter_lines()`.
• **No test coverage**: None of the new or existing tests in `tests/test_decoders.py` exercise any of the newly-supported separators to document or verify this broadened behaviour.

Recommendation: either restrict `splitlines()` by post-filtering (only split on `\n`, `\r`, `\r\n`), or update the docstring + add explicit tests that clearly document the expanded behaviour as intentional.

findings.title: No tests verify that chunks ending in non-\r newline chars (\x0b, \x85, \u2028, etc.) are not incorrectly buffered
findings.detail: The condition `text[-1] not in NEWLINE_CHARS` (line 288) determines whether the last segment from `splitlines()` is incomplete and should be buffered. For the newly-recognised newline characters (`\x0b`, `\x0c`, `\x1c`–`\x1e`, `\x85`, `\u2028`, `\u2029`) that are NOT `\r`, this branch should correctly NOT buffer the last element. However, this is entirely untested — there are no test cases that feed a chunk ending in one of these characters and verify the line is immediately emitted rather than withheld. Combined with the behavioural-expansion issue above, this represents a significant gap in test coverage for the new code paths.

findings.title: Repeated empty-chunk input when buffer ends with bare \r causes unbounded buffer growth (resource exhaustion)
findings.detail:
When `text[-1] == '\r'` (line 284), the code does `self.buffer = lines.pop() + '\r'`. This is correct for the `\r\n`-split-across-chunks case. However, if the caller repeatedly invokes `decode("")` while the buffer already holds a bare `'\r'`:

1. `decode("\r")` → `splitlines()` → `['']`, pop → `''`, buffer = `'\r'`, returns `[]`.
2. `decode("")` → `text = '\r'`, `splitlines()` → `['']`, pop → `''`, buffer = `'\r'` again — the buffer never drains.

While a well-behaved HTTP client will not produce repeated empty decode calls, a malicious or misbehaving server that sends isolated `\r` bytes followed by empty chunks can keep the buffer alive indefinitely without triggering a flush, which is a mild resource-exhaustion / denial-of-service vector.

Note: f1's concern about `lines.pop()` raising `IndexError` on an empty list is **not** a real risk because `splitlines()` on any non-empty string always returns at least one element.

Recommendation: Guard the `\r`-buffering branch so that if the reconstructed buffer is identical to what was already buffered (no progress), progress is forced — or document that callers must not feed repeated empty strings.

findings.title: NEWLINE_CHARS constant defined inside decode() method — should be at module or class scope
findings.detail: `NEWLINE_CHARS` is an invariant string that is reconstructed on every call to `decode()`. The rest of this file defines invariant data at module or class scope, and PEP 8 UPPER_CASE naming signals a module-level constant. Placing an UPPER_CASE name inside a method body is both an unusual style mix and a minor repeated allocation. Promote it to module scope (or at minimum class scope) to match the existing conventions.

findings.title: flush() behaviour change for buffered \r is untested and subtly different from the original
findings.detail: The original `flush()` converted a buffered trailing `\r` to a `\n`-terminated line. The new `flush()` calls `self.buffer.splitlines()`, which strips terminators entirely. While internally consistent with the new strip-terminators policy, the edge case where `self.buffer == "\r"` at flush time is not covered by any test. `"\r".splitlines()` returns `['']` (one empty string), not `[]`. Callers that previously relied on flush emitting a `\n`-terminated line will see different output. This should be explicitly tested and documented.

findings.title: Missing test: decode("") called with a non-empty buffer
findings.detail: There is no test that calls `decode("")` (empty string) when `self.buffer` already holds partial content. This path exercises the buffer-prepend logic (lines 273–280) and verifies the buffer is preserved and nothing is prematurely emitted. Adding such a test would also help guard against the infinite-loop scenario described in the resource-exhaustion finding.

findings.title: Typo in comment: "seperator" → "separator"
findings.detail: The comment on line 289 reads "newline seperator". The correct spelling is "separator". Please fix.

findings.title: Trailing \r buffering re-adds \r to already-stripped line from splitlines()
findings.detail:
When `text[-1] == '\r'`, the code does `self.buffer = lines.pop() + "\r"`. However, `str.splitlines()` already strips the line terminator from each element it returns, so `lines[-1]` does NOT end with `\r`. Re-appending `"\r"` is correct in intent (we need to hold the `\r` until we know whether the next chunk starts with `\n`). But the reconstructed buffer is `<line content> + "\r"`, which is fine.

The real bug is in the case where the buffered `\r` is NOT followed by `\n` — after the next decode call, the `\r`-terminated buffer gets prepended to the new text (line 276: `text = self.buffer + text`), so the combined text becomes `<prev content>\r<new text>`. Then `splitlines()` is called again and correctly splits on `\r`. This part works.

However, consider the edge case: input is split exactly at a `\r\n` boundary, e.g. chunk1 = `"a\r"`, chunk2 = `"\nb"`. After chunk1: `lines = ["a"]` from `splitlines()`, then `text[-1] == '\r'` so we do `self.buffer = lines.pop() + "\r"` → buffer = `"a\r"`. After chunk2: text = `"a\r" + "\nb"` = `"a\r\nb"`. `splitlines()` on `"a\r\nb"` gives `["a", "b"]` (splitlines treats `\r\n` as one separator). `text[-1]` is `"b"` which is not a newline char, so `self.buffer = lines.pop()` = `"b"`. Returns `["a"]`. This is correct.

Now consider chunk1 = `"a\r"`, chunk2 = `"\r"`, chunk3 = `"b"`. After chunk1: buffer = `"a\r"`. After chunk2: text = `"a\r\r"`. `splitlines()` → `["a", ""]`. `text[-1] == '\r'` → `self.buffer = lines.pop() + "\r"` = `"" + "\r"` = `"\r"`. Returns `["a"]`. After chunk3: text = `"\rb"`. `splitlines()` → `["", "b"]`. `text[-1]` is `"b"`, not newline → `self.buffer = "b"`. Returns `[""]`. Flush: `"b".splitlines()` → `["b"]`. Correct.

The truly problematic case: what if `lines` is empty when we try to `lines.pop()` at line 287 or 292? Consider chunk = `"\r"`. `splitlines()` on `"\r"` → `[""]`. `text[-1] == '\r'` → `self.buffer = lines.pop() + "\r"` = `"" + "\r"` = `"\r"`. Returns `[]`. OK.

Consider chunk = `"\n"`. `splitlines()` → `[""]`. `text[-1]` is `"\n"` which IS in NEWLINE_CHARS → no buffer pop. Returns `[""]`. Correct.

Actually the most critical bug: `lines` could be empty yet we still call `lines.pop()`. Consider input text (after prepending buffer) that consists of a single newline character at the end that is NOT `\r`, e.g. `"\n"`. `splitlines()` returns `[""]` — one element. `text[-1]` is `"\n"` which is in NEWLINE_CHARS, so no pop. Fine.

But consider `"\r"`: `splitlines()` returns `[""]`. `text[-1] == '\r'` → `lines.pop()` → returns `[]`. Fine.

What about completely empty text after buffer prepend? That's handled at line 279-280.

Actually there is a subtle logical flaw: when `text[-1] == '\r'`, the `\r` was already consumed as a line terminator by `splitlines()`, so the last element of `lines` is the content of the line TERMINATED by that `\r`. By popping it and adding `\r` back to the buffer, we are holding back a complete line unnecessarily when the next chunk doesn't start with `\n`. 

For example: chunk1=`"a\rb"`, chunk2=`"c"`. After chunk1: `splitlines("a\rb")` → `["a", "b"]`. `text[-1]` is `"b"`, not newline → `self.buffer = lines.pop()` = `"b"`. Returns `["a"]`. After chunk2: text=`"bc"`. `splitlines()` → `["bc"]`. Not newline at end → `self.buffer = "bc"`. Returns `[]`. Flush: `["bc"]`. Final output: `["a", "bc"]`. Correct.

Chunk1=`"a\r"`, chunk2=`"b"`. After chunk1: `splitlines()` → `["a"]`. `text[-1]=='\r'` → `self.buffer = lines.pop() + "\r"` = `"a\r"`. Returns `[]`. After chunk2: text=`"a\rb"`. `splitlines()` → `["a", "b"]`. `text[-1]`=`"b"` not newline → `self.buffer = "b"`. Returns `["a"]`. Flush: `["b"]`. Final: `["a", "b"]`. Correct.

So the `\r` boundary logic works. Let me look for the actual `lines` being empty when pop is called.

Edge: buffer=`"x\r"`, incoming text=`""`. After prepend: text=`"x\r"`, not empty. `splitlines("x\r")` → `["x"]`. `text[-1]=='\r'` → `self.buffer = "x" + "\r"` = `"x\r"`. Returns `[]`. This is an infinite-loop-like scenario (buffer never drains) if you keep sending empty strings, but it terminates correctly on flush.

Actually wait — what if after prepending the buffer the resulting text ends with `\r` and `splitlines()` returns an EMPTY list? Is that possible? `splitlines()` on a string ending with `\r` always returns at least one element (the `\r` itself counts as a line separator producing at least one line). E.g., `"\r".splitlines()` → `[""]`. So `lines` will not be empty when `text[-1] == '\r'`. Similarly for the non-newline case: if text is non-empty and `text[-1]` is not a newline char, `splitlines()` will include the last segment. So `lines.pop()` is always safe here.

Conclusion: The logic appears correct for the described use cases. However, the change in behavior (splitlines now supports more line-ending characters like \x0b, \x0c, \x1c, \x1d, \x1e, \x85, \u2028, \u2029) may be unintentional and could break callers that expect only \n, \r, \r\n to be treated as line separators.

findings.title: When text ends with \r and splitlines() returns empty list, lines.pop() raises IndexError
findings.detail: Although in practice `splitlines()` on a non-empty string always returns at least one element, so this is not actually a crash risk. The real issue is different: see the behavioral change bug below.

findings.title: LineDecoder now splits on many additional Unicode line-ending characters not in original spec
findings.detail:
The original implementation only handled `
`, ``, and `
`. The new implementation uses `str.splitlines()` which also splits on `\x0b` (vertical tab), `\x0c` (form feed), `\x1c`, `\x1d`, `\x1e`, `\x85` (NEL), ` ` (line separator), and ` ` (paragraph separator). These characters may appear legitimately in HTTP response bodies (e.g., JSON strings containing ` `/` `) and would now be incorrectly treated as line separators. The docstring still says 'supporting any of `
`, ``, or `
`' but the actual behavior is now much broader. This is a behavioral regression for callers relying on line-by-line iteration of HTTP responses that contain these characters.

findings.title: Non-newline trailing character check is insufficient for multi-char newlines consumed by splitlines()
findings.detail:
The condition `text[-1] not in NEWLINE_CHARS` is used to determine whether the last segment from `splitlines()` is incomplete and should be buffered. However, `NEWLINE_CHARS` only checks a single trailing character. The sequence `
` ends in `
` which IS in NEWLINE_CHARS, so `
`-terminated text won't buffer the last segment — that's correct. But what about text ending in one of the other multi-char sequences? All the other splitlines separators are single characters, so this is fine. The issue is only that NEWLINE_CHARS is broader than intended (see the previous finding).

findings.title: flush() no longer normalizes trailing \r to \n
findings.detail:
The original `flush()` converted a trailing `` to `
` (i.e., returned `[self.buffer[:-1] + '
']`). After the refactor, `flush()` calls `self.buffer.splitlines()` which strips all line terminators. The buffer at flush time could be `'sometext'` (because `decode()` explicitly stores the `` back into the buffer at line 287). `'sometext'.splitlines()` returns `['sometext']`, which is the correct behavior under the new stripping-terminators policy. However, if the intent is to match stdlib `splitlines()` semantics exactly (no terminators), this is consistent. This is a behavioral change from the original (which emitted `
`-terminated lines), and while consistent internally, may surprise users expecting ``-terminated output. This is more of a behavior change than a bug, but should be explicitly noted.

findings.title: NEWLINE_CHARS constant redefined on every decode() call
findings.detail: NEWLINE_CHARS is defined inside the decode() method body, so it is reconstructed as a new string object on every single call. This is a minor inefficiency but not a security issue. No security impact.

findings.title: Off-by-one in \r-boundary buffering causes incorrect data reconstruction and potential information leakage or data corruption
findings.detail: When text[-1] == '\r' (line 284), the code does `self.buffer = lines.pop() + "\r"`. However, `str.splitlines()` already consumes the trailing '\r' as a line separator, so the popped element is the content of that last line *without* the '\r'. Re-appending '\r' and storing in the buffer is the intended approach here — but consider the case where the '\r' is itself the entire input or follows an empty line. More critically: if `lines` is empty after splitlines() (e.g., text == "\r"), calling `lines.pop()` on an empty list raises an IndexError. For example, `decode("\r")` → splitlines() → `['']`... actually that is one element, but `decode("a\r")` → splitlines() → `['a']` which is fine. However `decode("\r")` → splitlines() → `['']` and pop gives `''`, buffer becomes `'\r'`. Then `decode("")` is called: buffer `'\r'` + `""` = `"\r"`, text[-1] == '\r', splitlines() of `"\r"` = `['']`, pop gives `''`, buffer = `'' + '\r'` = `'\r'` again — **infinite loop / unbounded buffer growth** if the caller keeps feeding empty strings. While this is not a direct injection risk, it represents a resource exhaustion / denial-of-service vector if an attacker can control the stream to send isolated '\r' bytes followed by repeated empty chunks. Consider guarding against this: only buffer when `lines` is non-empty after the pop, or check that the reconstructed buffer is not just '\r' in isolation.

findings.title: Expanded set of newline characters (Unicode line separators) may bypass server-sent event or HTTP header parsing expectations
findings.detail: By adopting Python's full `str.splitlines()` semantics, the decoder now treats U+2028 (LINE SEPARATOR), U+2029 (PARAGRAPH SEPARATOR), U+0085 (NEL), U+000B (VT), U+000C (FF), and several others as line boundaries. In an HTTP/SSE streaming context only \n, \r, and \r\n are valid line terminators per RFC 7230 and the SSE specification. An attacker-controlled response body containing these Unicode characters could craft lines that appear whole to the application but are split by the decoder, potentially causing header-injection-style confusion in line-oriented protocol parsers layered on top of iter_lines(). Callers who process SSE `data:` fields or similar line-based protocols may misinterpret the split output.

findings.title: Module-level constant defined inside method body
findings.detail: NEWLINE_CHARS is a constant string that never changes, but it is re-created on every call to decode(). The existing code in this file defines class-level or module-level names rather than embedding invariant data inside methods. Move NEWLINE_CHARS to module scope (or at least class scope) to match that convention and avoid the repeated allocation.

findings.title: Constant name uses UPPER_CASE but is defined inside a function
findings.detail: PEP 8 UPPER_CASE naming signals a module-level constant. Placing an UPPER_CASE name inside a method body is an unusual style mix. If the constant is kept inside the method it should be lower_case (e.g. newline_chars); if it is promoted to module scope the current name is fine.

findings.title: Typo in comment: "seperator" should be "separator"
findings.detail: The comment on line 289 reads "newline seperator" — "separator" is the correct spelling. The surrounding code and the rest of the file use standard English spelling.

findings.title: No tests for new Unicode/vertical-tab newline characters in LineDecoder
findings.detail:
The new implementation explicitly lists `NEWLINE_CHARS = "
\x0b\x0c\x1c\x1d\x1e\x85  "` and delegates to `str.splitlines()`, which recognises all of these. However, none of the updated tests in `tests/test_decoders.py` exercise any of the newly-supported separators (\x0b vertical tab, \x0c form feed, \x1c–\x1e file/group/record separators, \x85 NEL,   line separator,   paragraph separator). The old implementation only handled `
`, ``, and `
`; the new one deliberately expands this set, but zero test cases verify that behaviour.

findings.title: No test for trailing Unicode/other newline chars at chunk boundary in decode()
findings.detail: The buffer-management logic in `decode()` has two branches: one for a trailing `\r` (line 284) and one for any non-newline trailing character (line 288). There are no tests that verify the second branch is NOT entered (i.e., that a chunk ending in one of the non-`\r` newline characters like `\x0b`, `\x85`, `\u2028`, etc. is immediately emitted rather than buffered). A chunk ending in `\x0b` should NOT be buffered, but this is untested.

findings.title: flush() with a trailing \r in the buffer is no longer tested
findings.detail: The old `flush()` had explicit logic to handle a buffered trailing `\r` (converting it to `\n`). The new `flush()` simply calls `self.buffer.splitlines()`. The existing `test_line_decoder_cr` test only checks `flush()` after a trailing `\r` that was NOT followed by `\n` in subsequent chunks (the `baz\r` case), but doesn't test `flush()` when the entire remaining buffer is just `\r` by itself. With the new implementation, `"\r".splitlines()` returns `['']` (an empty string), not `[]`. This edge case — `flush()` called with `self.buffer == "\r"` — is not covered.

findings.title: decode() with an empty string after prepending a non-empty buffer is untested
findings.detail: The path where `self.buffer` is non-empty and the incoming `text` argument is `""` (empty string) exercises the prepend-and-check logic (lines 273-280). While `decode("")` is tested when the buffer is empty, there is no test that calls `decode("")` when the buffer already holds partial content, to confirm the buffer is preserved and nothing is emitted.

findings.title: splitlines() expands recognised line endings beyond \n/\r/\r\n, introducing behavioural regression and potential security concern
findings.detail:
The new implementation delegates to `str.splitlines()`, which treats `\x0b` (VT), `\x0c` (FF), `\x1c`–`\x1e`, `\x85` (NEL), `\u2028` (LINE SEPARATOR), and `\u2029` (PARAGRAPH SEPARATOR) as line boundaries. The original code — and the docstring — only guaranteed `\n`, `\r`, and `\r\n`. HTTP/1.1 (RFC 7230) and the SSE specification only recognise those three as valid line terminators. Concretely:

• **Behavioural regression**: JSON bodies or other response payloads that legitimately contain `\u2028`/`\u2029` (which are valid in JSON strings) will be incorrectly split into multiple lines.
• **Security concern**: An attacker-controlled response body containing these characters could craft lines that appear whole to the application but are split by the decoder, potentially causing header-injection-style confusion in any line-oriented protocol parser (e.g., SSE `data:` fields) layered on top of `iter_lines()`.
• **No test coverage**: None of the new or existing tests in `tests/test_decoders.py` exercise any of the newly-supported separators to document or verify this broadened behaviour.

Recommendation: either restrict `splitlines()` by post-filtering (only split on `\n`, `\r`, `\r\n`), or update the docstring + add explicit tests that clearly document the expanded behaviour as intentional.

findings.title: No tests verify that chunks ending in non-\r newline chars (\x0b, \x85, \u2028, etc.) are not incorrectly buffered
findings.detail: The condition `text[-1] not in NEWLINE_CHARS` (line 288) determines whether the last segment from `splitlines()` is incomplete and should be buffered. For the newly-recognised newline characters (`\x0b`, `\x0c`, `\x1c`–`\x1e`, `\x85`, `\u2028`, `\u2029`) that are NOT `\r`, this branch should correctly NOT buffer the last element. However, this is entirely untested — there are no test cases that feed a chunk ending in one of these characters and verify the line is immediately emitted rather than withheld. Combined with the behavioural-expansion issue above, this represents a significant gap in test coverage for the new code paths.

findings.title: Repeated empty-chunk input when buffer ends with bare \r causes unbounded buffer growth (resource exhaustion)
findings.detail:
When `text[-1] == '\r'` (line 284), the code does `self.buffer = lines.pop() + '\r'`. This is correct for the `\r\n`-split-across-chunks case. However, if the caller repeatedly invokes `decode("")` while the buffer already holds a bare `'\r'`:

1. `decode("\r")` → `splitlines()` → `['']`, pop → `''`, buffer = `'\r'`, returns `[]`.
2. `decode("")` → `text = '\r'`, `splitlines()` → `['']`, pop → `''`, buffer = `'\r'` again — the buffer never drains.

While a well-behaved HTTP client will not produce repeated empty decode calls, a malicious or misbehaving server that sends isolated `\r` bytes followed by empty chunks can keep the buffer alive indefinitely without triggering a flush, which is a mild resource-exhaustion / denial-of-service vector.

Note: f1's concern about `lines.pop()` raising `IndexError` on an empty list is **not** a real risk because `splitlines()` on any non-empty string always returns at least one element.

Recommendation: Guard the `\r`-buffering branch so that if the reconstructed buffer is identical to what was already buffered (no progress), progress is forced — or document that callers must not feed repeated empty strings.

findings.title: NEWLINE_CHARS constant defined inside decode() method — should be at module or class scope
findings.detail: `NEWLINE_CHARS` is an invariant string that is reconstructed on every call to `decode()`. The rest of this file defines invariant data at module or class scope, and PEP 8 UPPER_CASE naming signals a module-level constant. Placing an UPPER_CASE name inside a method body is both an unusual style mix and a minor repeated allocation. Promote it to module scope (or at minimum class scope) to match the existing conventions.

findings.title: flush() behaviour change for buffered \r is untested and subtly different from the original
findings.detail: The original `flush()` converted a buffered trailing `\r` to a `\n`-terminated line. The new `flush()` calls `self.buffer.splitlines()`, which strips terminators entirely. While internally consistent with the new strip-terminators policy, the edge case where `self.buffer == "\r"` at flush time is not covered by any test. `"\r".splitlines()` returns `['']` (one empty string), not `[]`. Callers that previously relied on flush emitting a `\n`-terminated line will see different output. This should be explicitly tested and documented.

findings.title: Missing test: decode("") called with a non-empty buffer
findings.detail: There is no test that calls `decode("")` (empty string) when `self.buffer` already holds partial content. This path exercises the buffer-prepend logic (lines 273–280) and verifies the buffer is preserved and nothing is prematurely emitted. Adding such a test would also help guard against the infinite-loop scenario described in the resource-exhaustion finding.

findings.title: Typo in comment: "seperator" → "separator"
findings.detail: The comment on line 289 reads "newline seperator". The correct spelling is "separator". Please fix.
